coredata.dc:title: Sulfur-rich carbon dots as a novel fluorescent imaging probe for distinguishing the pathological changes of mouse-bone cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Pengcheng
Lyu, Dandan
Shen, Pei Kang
Wang, Xinxing
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, the florescent carbon dots (C-dots), as a type of heavy-metal-free nanomaterials, have been widely suggested for biological imaging, but attempts made for detecting and distinguishing bone-related diseases are still rarely reported by previous works. Here, a type of C-dots, featuring rich sulfur content and excellent bone cell affinity, is prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method. Various characterization techniques have been employed to study the structure and fluorescent properties of the sulfur C-dots, which include the X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption, and fluorescence spectra. Our results show that the sulfur C-dots are featured with small particle size at ~4 nm and rich sulfur content as high as 24.85%, allowing the C-dots to exhibit excellent stability in the aqueous solution and good compatibility and affinity with bone cells. Moreover, we find the sulfur rich C-dots as the excitation wavelength changes from 270 nm to 460 nm can exhibit emission-tunable colors from blue, green and to yellow, showing a rich choice of fluorescent colors for cell imaging. Together with the advantages of low cytotoxicity behavior and sulfur-rich functionalized groups, the sulfur C-dots have been proposed to act as a fluorescent probe for detecting and distinguishing bone-related diseased cells where bone-related normal and diseased cells after incubated with sulfur rich C-dots are found to emit blue and yellow fluorescence upon excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively. Unlike previous works that are always reported to use the C-dots for cell imaging, here we have presented new application possibilities of C-dots for distinguishing the diseased cells from the normal cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Sulfur
One pot method
Tunable fluorescence
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the targeted sulfur doped carbon dots
UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectral analysis
Analysis on the toxicity of sulfur rich C-dots, HeLa cell imaging and bone-diseased cell detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Basic Research Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work is supported by the National Basic Research Program of China ( 2015CB932304 ), the Major International (Regional) Joint Research Project ( 51210002 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2015A030312007 ), the Guangxi Science and Technology Project ( AB16380030 ), and the Danish Initiative Towards Non-precious Metal Polymer Fuel Cells ( 4106-000012B ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of the as-prepared sulfur C-dots; (b) low-resolution and high-resolution (inset (i)) TEM profile of the sulfur C-dots and correlated particle size distribution (inset (ii)) as well as Elemental mapping of one selected sulfur C-dots particle (inset (iii)); (c) FTIR spectrum and (d) full XPS spectrum of the sulfur C-dots.

(a) UV–vis absorption (curve 1) and fluorescent spectra upon excitation at 250–490 nm (curve 2–10); (b) Dependence of time on the 360 nm-excited fluorescent intensity of the sulfur rich C-dots after kept in the aqueous solution (curve 1) and boiled water (curve 2); (c) and (d) Dependence of excitation wavelength on the CIE chromaticity coordinates of the sulfur rich C-dots, the images shown in the insets are the digital photographs of the sulfur rich C-dots upon excitation at different wavelengths derived from a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 fluorescent spectrophotometer.

(a) Dependence of incubation time on the viability of HeLa cell after cultured at 0.80 mg/mL of the as-prepared sulfur C-dots; (b) Confocal fluorescent microscope images of HeLa cell taken upon excitation with bright field (i), 405 nm (ii) and 488 nm (iii) semiconductor laser; (c) Confocal fluorescent microscope images of mouse bone-related normal (i) and diseased cells (ii) taken upon excitation with a 405 nm (i) and 488 nm (ii) semiconductor laser, (iii) is the confocal fluorescent microscope image that simultaneously involves the mouse bone-related normal and diseased cells.

head.ce:title: Sulfur-rich carbon dots as a novel fluorescent imaging probe for distinguishing the pathological changes of mouse-bone cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Sustainable Energy Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Hangzhou 310058, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Collaborative Innovation Center of Sustainable Energy Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Sustainable Energy Materials, Guangxi University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Dr. Pengcheng Zhu, and Dr. Dandan Lyu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, the florescent carbon dots (C-dots), as a type of heavy-metal-free nanomaterials, have been widely suggested for biological imaging, but attempts made for detecting and distinguishing bone-related diseases are still rarely reported by previous works. Here, a type of C-dots, featuring rich sulfur content and excellent bone cell affinity, is prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method. Various characterization techniques have been employed to study the structure and fluorescent properties of the sulfur C-dots, which include the X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption, and fluorescence spectra. Our results show that the sulfur C-dots are featured with small particle size at ~4 nm and rich sulfur content as high as 24.85%, allowing the C-dots to exhibit excellent stability in the aqueous solution and good compatibility and affinity with bone cells. Moreover, we find the sulfur rich C-dots as the excitation wavelength changes from 270 nm to 460 nm can exhibit emission-tunable colors from blue, green and to yellow, showing a rich choice of fluorescent colors for cell imaging. Together with the advantages of low cytotoxicity behavior and sulfur-rich functionalized groups, the sulfur C-dots have been proposed to act as a fluorescent probe for detecting and distinguishing bone-related diseased cells where bone-related normal and diseased cells after incubated with sulfur rich C-dots are found to emit blue and yellow fluorescence upon excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively. Unlike previous works that are always reported to use the C-dots for cell imaging, here we have presented new application possibilities of C-dots for distinguishing the diseased cells from the normal cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, our living standard as the society development develops has been improved greatly as compared to the 1940s, but a large number of health problems, such as the bone-related diseases and dysfunctions that result from the change of diseased cells, also appear. On the basis of the data derived from a lot of frequently-reported research results and clinical statistics [1–5], the low bone mass, weaken bones and correlated impair normal functions (e.g., osteoporosis [6]) are considered as the most sticky bone-related diseases and dysfunctions. So far there is still no a feasible medical strategy to cure the osteoporosis, because this type of bone-related disease cannot reproduce the mineral bone but only mitigates the mineral bone degradation [4–6]. Although some detection techniques, such as X-ray and computed tomography (CT) [7–9], have been already developed and widely suggested for detecting the clinical bone-related diseases and dysfunctions, great harms, resulting from the high energy electrons of X-ray ray and CT ionizing radiation, can impose on the patients. Moreover, once the patients are exposed to the high energy electrons for a long time, other diseases, such as Leukemia disease [10], may appear. Therefore, exploring an effective yet non-invasive detection method becomes highly of scientific importance.

Over the past years, the optical imaging strategies based on the fluorescent materials have played a significant role in the fields of biology and medicine [11,12], and some of them have been used for imaging-guided surgery [12–15]. So far, a large number of highly fluorescent materials, such as the organic dyes [16], semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., CdSe [17], CdTe [18], PbTe [19], CdS [20]), mechano-optical converters [21], down-conversion and up-conversion nanoparticles [22–27], semiconducting polymer nanoparticles [28], metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [29], have been widely reported for vivo optical imaging application. The above fluorescent materials, however, usually suffer from either high cytotoxicity and cost (i.e., organic dyes and semiconductor QDs), large particle size (usually micro-size, i.e., mechano-optical converters), single emission color (i.e., trivalent rare earth (RE) doped down-conversion and up-conversion nanoparticles, e.g., Eu3+, Tb3+, Er3+, Yb3+, Nd3+ etc) or ultrahigh molecular weight and highly complicated synthesis process (i.e., metal organic compounds (MOCs) and metal organic frameworks (MOFs)). All the disadvantages actually will hinder their future development toward biological purposes, typically if one wants to employ only one type of these fluorescent materials to realize multiple biological purposes.

Unlike the above-listed fluorescent materials, C-dots, as a type of heavy-metal-free nanomaterials, feature the advantages of excitation-dependent tunable-emissions [28], large-scale production due to low cost [29], controllable particle size from several nanometers to dozens nanometers [30], and facile synthesis strategy [31]. Typically, once the exotic heteroatoms, such as nitrogen [32], sulfur [33], and boron [34], are introduced into the C-dots, the optical and electronic properties of the C-dots can be changed significantly, giving the C-dots more functions and broader opportunities for different practical applications. For example, doping of the C-dots with the sulfur can lead to enhanced florescent intensity, thereby allowing to motivating the biomedical imaging and cationic cytotoxicity determination toward higher quality and sensitivity [35–37]. As a result, the C-dots and their counterparts have received continuous attentions in the recent years. In addition to the above-mentioned behaviors, the excitation-dependent emissions of the C-dots benefit us selecting desired emission wavelength for different purposes. For sulfur doped C-dots, they usually feature excellent binding ability due to sulfur-related groups adsorbed onto the surface of the C-dots.

Enlightened by the above-mentioned discussions, typically the multiple colors resulting from the excitation-dependent tunable emissions and the binding ability of sulfur doped C-dots, here we fabricated and reported a type of sulfur C-dots by using the one-pot hydrothermal method. In order to characterize the phase-purity, microstructural information and fluorescent properties, techniques, such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and fluorescent spectra, were performed on the sulfur C-dots. Our findings showed that the sulfur C-dots featured the advantages of small size (~4 nm), rich sulfur content (~24.85%) and sulfur-related groups, broad excitation-dependent emissions, low cytotoxicity and good stability. The excitation-dependent tunable emissions and sulfur-related groups allowed the C-dots to exhibit a rich choice of fluorescent colors for imaging cells and strong adsorption with bone cells, respectively. This could allow us to perform the experiment concerning the adsorption interaction between the C-dots and bone-related cells, with the purpose of detecting, localizing and distinguishing the bone-related diseased cells from normal cells. We expect this work not only can provide some new insights into how to use the spectral properties of the C-dots to dig out new biomedical imaging applications, but also opens up more application possibilities of the C-dots and their related nanomaterials and nanocomposites toward detection, diagnosis and location of other types of cells and their associated diseases in the future, not just limited to bone-related cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the targeted sulfur doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the one-pot hydrothermal strategy was used to prepare the sulfur rich C-dots [38,39]. Specifically, the purified water, featuring a surface tension of 50.1 mN m−1 and a resistivity of 10 MΩ cm−1 at 20.0 ± 0.5 °C, was obtained from a Modulab 2015 water purification system (San Antonio, TX). The methionine and ethylene diamine were used as the co-precursors. We first prepared the samples with various ratios (i.e., 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:1.5, 1:2 and 1:2.5) of methionine and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). During this process, 5 mmol of methionine and ethylene diamine was dissolved into 10 mL of deionized water. After stirring the dissolved water, the solutions in form of clear and homogeneous feature were obtained. Then, we transferred the mixture solutions into a 50 mL steel reaction kettle and kept them at 250 °C in an oven for 10 h. As a result, the initial solution, which was in form of deep yellow, could be achieved. With a final drying under vacuum condition for 48 h and a subsequent dialysis membrane (MWCO = 3000 Da) for another 48 h, the sulfur C-dots were obtained. At last, the acids were neutralized with sodium hydroxide, followed by removing the resulting salts through the crystallization in the cold water.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of sulfur C-dots was recorded on a Bruker diffractometer utilizing Cu Kα as the radiation source (λ = 1.5418 Å). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the sulfur rich C-dots were performed by using a VG Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific) and JEOL 1200X TEM, respectively. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescent spectra of sulfur C-dots were recorded by the Varian Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3), respectively. The zeta potential of sulfur C-dots in the aqueous solution was analyzed by a Zetasizer Nano ZS System (Malvern, Inc., UK) equipped with a 633 nm laser source. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of C-dots were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer 100 FTIR spectrometer (Frontier). A confocal microscopy equipped with a 405 and 488 nm laser was used to image the sulfur rich C-dots incubated mice bone-related normal and disease cells.

Fig. 1(a) shows the XRD pattern of the as-prepared C-dots. A broad XRD peak dominated at 23.5° is observed. The XRD results revealed that we have achieved the targeted C-dots phase after compared with other sulfur-related C-dots (e.g., 23.7° and 23.2° in nitrogen [30] and sulfur doped C-dots [34], respectively). The TEM profile of Fig. 1(b) shows the sulfur C-dots are featured with uniform particle distribution. The HR TEM image of one selected particle shows that the lattice constant of the sulfur C-dots is 0.237 nm (the inset (ii) of Fig. 1(b)). The average particle size, calculating from 200 particles of the sulfur C-dots, is ~4 nm (the inset (ii) of Fig. 1(b)). In addition, we also carried out the elemental mapping on one selected particle (inset (iii) of Fig. 1(c)). Obviously, the sulfur C-dots consist of the S, C, and O element. Our EDS analysis revealed that the sulfur content of the sulfur C-dots is as high as 24.85%, which is much higher than those S/C content reported by previous works, such as 5.08% of sulfur contents in N,S doped C-dots [38] and 5.4% of sulfur content in N,S doped C-dots [40]. Such high sulfur content of our sulfur C-dots may lead to good water solubility and adsorption ability.

Furthermore, we also carried out the FTIR measurement, with the aim of revealing the functional groups attached onto the surface of the sulfur C-dots, and the results are shown in Fig. 1(c). Obviously, the FTIR results reveal that there are a lot of chemical functional groups in the sulfur C-dots, such as the O-H (3435 cm−1), C-N (3223 cm−1), C-H (2973 cm−1), S-H (2498 cm−1), C˭C (1651 cm−1), C-N (1467 cm−1), C˭O (1097 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1), and C-S (1220 cm−1) groups. These chemical elements, i.e., carbon (C), sulfur (S), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O), are confirmed further by the XPS spectrum (Fig. 1(d)), and they are located at 284.9 eV for C 1s, 164.2 eV for S 2p and 228.9 eV for S 2s, 400.0 eV for N 1s, and 532.5 eV for O 1s, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectral analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2(a)(curve 1) shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of sulfur rich C-dots. It is shown in this figure that two absorption bands, which are resulted from the n-π* transition of C˭O bond (i.e., 268 nm) and π-π* transition (i.e., 358 nm) of C˭C bond [30,38,40–42], are observed within the range of 240–500 nm. In view of this absorption range, we measured the fluorescent spectra of sulfur rich C-dots upon excitation at the range of 250–490 nm. Because of the regularly shifting variation of the dominated emission bands (Fig. 2(a)(curves 2–10)), the sulfur C-dots as the excitation wavelength increases from 250 nm to 490 nm shows the emission-tunable colors with the sequence of green → blue → green → yellow. Such tunable-emissions can be vividly reflected by the Commission International d′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 2(c-d)) and their correlated digital photographs achieved by different excitation wavelengths (the inset of (Fig. 2(c))). Typically, we found the sulfur rich C-dots after kept in the aqueous solution for 20 days (curve 1) and/or boiled water for 20 h (curve 2) can still exhibit the bright emission intensity as compared to its initial emission intensity upon exposure to a 365 nm lamp (see Fig. 2(b)). This indicates that the sulfur rich C-dots hold excellent stability against thermally -induced degradation in the aqueous solution, which is due to the existence of abundant hydrophilic (-OH) and carboxylic groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis on the toxicity of sulfur rich C-dots, HeLa cell imaging and bone-diseased cell detection
ce:section.ce:para: As vividly shown by the florescent results of Fig. 2, we can easily find the sulfur rich C-dots are featured with multiple emission colors upon different excitation wavelengths, thus exhibiting a rich choice of fluorescent colors for different cell imaging. This is because different types of cells may exhibit different responses of their sensitivity to different fluorescent colors. On basis of this concept and the problems encountered in the bone-related diseased cells, it would be good if we could use the as-prepared sulfur rich C-dots to distinguish different types of cells. To verify this proposal, we tentatively carried out the experiment that involved whether our sulfur C-dots could really distinguish the bone-related disease cells from the normal cells.

Prior to the experiment proposed above, we have verified the toxicity of the sulfur rich C-dots by using the HeLa cells. Prior to the cytotoxicity evaluation, we routinely cultured HeLa cells were in flasks and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified hood incubator set at 5% CO2 atmosphere. And then, the HeLa cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well in a 96-well plate. After 24 h, the HeLa cells were cultured with a 0.80 mg/mL of the sulfur C-dots and then monitored their survival viability at different time ranging from 0 h to 30 h. As revealed by Fig. 3(a), we can easily find that the HeLa cells after cultured with 0.80 mg/mL of sulfur rich C-dots for 24 h can basically exhibit the viability analogous to the initial HeLa cells. The viability number of HeLa cells can still remain above 90%, indicating that the sulfur rich C-dots feature low cytotoxicity and thus can be used for the biological imaging experiment. As revealed by a confocal fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 3(b)), upon excitation at different laser sources (e.g., 405 nm and 488 nm), the HeLa cells stained by the sulfur rich C-dots can show two different fluorescent colors analogous to the spectral results and digital photographs achieved at 405 nm and 488 nm (Fig. 2(c)). On one hand, the imaging results have proved that the sulfur C-dots can enter into and stain evenly the membrane and cytoplasmic area of the HeLa cells. On the other hand, the results confirm the proposal we proposed above, i.e., the existence of multiple choices of fluorescent colors that can image the same type of cells.

Inspired by the features of small particle size, non-toxicity, rich sulfur and responses of the same cells to different imaging ability, we have carried out the research concerning the binding affinity ability of the sulfur rich C-dots with the mouse bone-related diseased and normal cells, the aim of which is to study whether the two types of bone-related cells could show their sensitive response to different fluorescence of sulfur C-dots. We first collected the mouse embryos after a natural spawn within a period of 8 h, and then grown it for a 6-days fertilization. And then, 30 mL of buffered embryo media contained 0.4% of Tricaine were anaesthetized in the larvae, followed by injecting the abdominal cavity with 5 μL of sulfur rich C-dots (5 mg L−1). With these steps, the survived larvae, recovering from the embryo media for 30 min, were grown for the experiment. It has to mention that the fresh embryo media need to replace every two days.

With the above biological experiments, we had injected 10 μL of 5 mg L−1 sulfur rich C-dots into the abdominal cavity of the larvae to test whether the C-dots can bind with the bone-related bone. After two days of cultivation, we began to analyze the deposition, affinity and specificity ability according to the unique excitation-dependent fluorescent color of the sulfur rich C-dots. In Fig. 3(c), we can observe two types of fluorescent colors from the bone-related cell. Green and yellow fluorescence, deriving from the sulfur rich C-dots cultured bone-related normal (i) and diseased cells (ii), are exhibited upon excitation at both 405 nm and 488 nm. The yellow fluorescence, which basically is the same as that of Fig. 2, cannot be exhibited upon excitation at the laser wavelength of 405 nm. Such situation, however, is reversed when the C-dots cultured diseased cells are performed. In Fig. 3(c)(ii), the sulfur C-dots cultured diseased cells upon excitation at 488 nm show the yellow fluorescence analogous to the results of Fig. 2, but they cannot exhibit any fluorescence upon excitation at 405 nm. When the bone-related diseased and normal cells are simultaneously mixed with each other, upon excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm enables exhibiting two types of blue and yellow florescence (Fig. 3(c)(iii)). With a very careful inspection, we can find that there are some normal cells with the cell morphologies analogous to Fig. 3(c)(i) and some diseased cells with the cell morphologies analogous to Fig. 3(c)(ii). More specifically, the imaging results of normal cells of Fig. 3(c)(iii) and (c)(i) have the same morphologies, but once these normal cells are diseased, the morphologies will change (Fig. 3(c)(ii)). Hence, this is a direct, easily and also very simple method, typically when we have already known there are two types of different cells but they can show different response to different fluorescence. The results indicate that our sulfur C-dots have the ability to several as a probe technique for detecting the bone-related disease cells and distinguish them from the normal cells.

In this work, we synthesized a type of sulfur rich C-dots by using one-pot hydrothermal method. Our findings revealed that such C-dots not only contained the sulfur element as high as ~25% but also could exhibit excitation-dependent emission-tunable colors with the sequence of green, blue, green and yellow. Together with the features of small particle size (~4 nm), good stability in the aqueous solution and low cytotoxicity, our HeLa cell imaging experiment revealed such C-dots upon excitation at 405 nm and 488 nm could exhibit blue and yellow fluorescence analogous to the fluorescent colors of the sulfur C-dots in the aqueous solution, respectively. The HeLa imaging results, working together with high binding ability of the sulfur C-dots with mouse bone-related cells, allowed us to successfully propose a concept, i.e., using the C-dots to detect and locate the bone-related disease cells as well as distinguish them from the normal cells. This work opened up a new strategy for the C-dots for not only biomedical imaging but also detection and location of bone-related cells and their associated diseases in the feature.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (2015CB932304), the Major International (Regional) Joint Research Project (51210002), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2015A030312007), the Guangxi Science and Technology Project (AB16380030), and the Danish Initiative Towards Non-precious Metal Polymer Fuel Cells (4106-000012B).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest with the contents of this article.
